I think about how I could have handled things differently, and it makes me feel so inadequate. I just don’t want to let anyone down, but it feels like I’m always on the verge of disappointing myself too.

patient.query: When I think about it, I just get so frustrated with myself. I keep imagining all the things I could have said or done to make it better. It’s like I spiral into this harsh self-talk, telling myself I should have known better or that I should have been more prepared. And then I feel this weight of shame—it’s like I can’t let myself forgive what happened.

patient.query: I think it comes from my upbringing, you know? My parents always had high expectations, and I felt like I had to prove myself at every turn. I guess I internalized this idea that I needed to be the best, or at least not make any mistakes. So now, when I mess up, it feels like it reflects who I am as a person, like I’m just letting everyone down again. It’s exhausting, and sometimes I wonder if I can really keep up with those expectations

triggers: feeling like I'm letting everyone down

I can't make any mistakes
messing up reflects who I am

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

It’s really tough to shake
